segments.s718-g49: بزن is the imperative of زدن ('strike/drive in'), placed at the clause start as an order. کارد is the direct object 'knife/dagger' placed after the verb (classical word order allows verb-first imperatives). نافش is ناف + possessive suffix -ش 'his navel' (ناف + his). سراسر is an adverb 'all the way / right through' modifying the following verb phrase; بدر here functions as a spatial/completive element (poetic) after سراسر. The clause is elliptical: ‘Drive in the knife and (cut) his navel all the way through.’
segments.s718-g50: وزان = وز + آن (poetic compound) 'and/from that' linking this clause to the effect of the previous action. پس = 'then/therefore' (temporal/consequence). بجه‌گر is a compound noun meaning 'wet‑nurse / nursemaid' and is the beneficiary/goal in the next phrase. بیابی is the 2nd person present/subjunctive/optative form ('you will find' or 'you may find'); here it expresses the expected result ('you will find'). گذر is a noun 'passage / way'. Together: 'and from that, then, you will find a passage to the wet‑nurse.'
segments.s718-g51: هر آنکس که ... بشنود is a free relative: 'whoever / anyone who ...'. هر = 'every/any', آنکس = 'that person / one who'. که introduces the relative clause. آواز او = 'his voice' (possessive), and بشنود is the 3rd‑person present/subjunctive of شنیدن used in such generic relative clauses to mean 'hears' / 'may hear' (classical register).
segments.s718-g52: ز پیش = 'from/in front of / before' (poetic preposition phrase) and here indicates position relative to the سهبد (spahbed, a commander). به آخر دود = 'to the end of the smoke' with به + noun phrase marking direction/limit (به = 'to/toward', آخر = 'end', دود = 'smoke'). The whole phrase describes movement away (or to the limit) in front of the spahbed.
segments.s718-g53: یکی سوی فرش و یکی سوی گنج is a paired expression meaning 'one (goes) toward the carpet and another toward the treasure.' یکی here is an indefinite 'one / someone' (repeated for contrast). سوی = 'toward' marks direction, فرش = 'carpet' (here a metonym for the domestic/household place), and گنج = 'treasure/treasury' (the other destination). The verb is omitted (elliptical) and understood from context.
segments.s718-g54: نیاید is the negative present/subjunctive/impersonal form 'does not come / shall not come'. ز introduces cause/reason 'from / because of'. کشتن is the infinitive/masdar 'killing' (here the source of the supposed harm). بروی is 2nd‑person present/subjunctive of رفتن used impersonally here with رنج to mean 'fall upon / come to (you)'; تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun 'you'. رنج is the noun 'suffering / harm'. Together: 'no suffering will come to you from killing.'
segments.s718-g55: وگر = 'if / but if' introducing a conditional. خود ('self') emphasizes the agent 'they themselves' (reflexive/emphatic). کشندت is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive of کشتن plus enclitic pronoun -ت 'you' → 'they kill you' (classical subjunctive in a conditional). جهاندیده‌ای is a predicative adjective/noun phrase 'a worldly‑wise one / one who has seen the world' with the 2nd‑person ending -ای: 'you are worldly‑wise' (a stative comment linked to the conditional).
segments.s718-g56: همه = 'all / completely'; نیک و بدها = 'the goods and the bads' (نیک = good, بدها = bad things, pluralized). پسندیده‌ای is the perfect/passive/adjectival form built from پسندیدن with 2nd‑person ending 'you have approved / you are one who approves/accepts'; here it describes the addressee's disposition: you have accepted or judged both good and bad. Word order places the generalizing adverb 'همه' before the contrasted noun pair.
segments.s718-g41: همان is a demonstrative ('that/the same') placed before the noun; کارد is the noun 'knife/dagger'. در آستین is a prepositional phrase 'in the sleeve' (در + آستین). برهنه is an adjective following the noun phrase meaning 'bare/exposed' and here qualifies the dagger as kept uncovered in the sleeve.
segments.s718-g42: همی‌دار is an archaic/higher-register imperative or hortative 'hold/keep it'. تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until'. خواندت here functions as a 2nd-person finite verb 'you summon/call' (archaic/poetic form). یک‌تنه is an adverbial 'alone/single-handedly' qualifying the act of summoning.
segments.s718-g43: چو is the temporal/conditional conjunction 'when'. نزدیک is an adverb/adjective 'near'. چوبینه is an adjective meaning 'wooden' (describing the place or platform). آیی is the 2nd-person present/future 'you come/approach'. فراز is an adverb/verb-like direction 'up/above' (here implying 'go up' or 'ascend').
segments.s718-g44: چنین (thus) introduces the reported formula; گوی is an imperative/command 'say' (poetic form). کان is an archaic demonstrative/particle pointing to 'that' (here introducing the following noun). دختر is 'maiden/daughter' and سرفراز is an adjective 'proud/noble' describing her.
segments.s718-g45: مرا گفت is 'he said to me' (مرا = 'to me' object, گفت = 'said' in past). چون here is 'when/if' introducing the condition. راز is 'secret'. گویی is a verbal form 'you speak/you tell' (poetic/present subjunctive/imperative flavor). بگوش is the adverbial phrase 'to the ear / in the ear' (i.e., whispering or privately).
segments.s718-g46: سخنها is the plural noun 'words/talks'. ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. بیگانه مردم is a compound meaning 'strange/foreign people' or 'strangers'. بپوش is an imperative 'cover/conceal' (here 'keep hidden'). The whole segment commands hiding the speech from strangers.
segments.s718-g47: چو is 'when/if'. گوید is the present/medieval-style 'she says/asks' (used to report her utterance). چه رازست is the quoted question 'what is the secret?' (چه = 'what', رازست = 'is a secret' / 'is the secret'). با من بگوی is an imperative instruction 'say it to me' or 'tell her to come to me' (با + من = 'to me/with me', بگوی = 'say/tell' in imperative/optative form).
segments.s718-g48: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. بشتاب is the imperative 'hurry!'. و is the conjunction 'and'. نزدیک here functions as direction/adverb 'near' or 'approach' (poetic economy). بهرام is the proper name 'Bahram'. پوی is an archaic/poetic verb/adjective-root meaning 'go/seek/approach' (here part of the instruction 'go near Bahram').
segments.s718-g65: همانا is an emphatic particle meaning 'truly' or 'verily'. مرا is the dative/experiencer clitic 'to me' (here marking who is affected). سال بر صد is an idiomatic numerical phrase 'a year to a hundred' = 'a hundred years', and رسید is the past 3rd-person singular 'reached' or 'came upon' (i.e., 'have come upon me'). Word order places the experiencer before the temporal phrase and the verb at the end, typical of epic Persian.
segments.s718-g66: به بیچارگی is a prepositional phrase 'into/with misery' (here meaning 'in my misery'); چند is an interrogative 'how long / how much'; خواهم is the auxiliary indicating future/first person ('I will') and کشید is the lexical verb from کشیدن used here in the sense 'to endure/suffer' (literally 'I will draw/drag [this misery]'). Together they form the question 'how long will I endure this misery?'.
segments.s718-g67: فدای تو is a phrase 'sacrifice for you' (فدای used predicatively). بادا is an optative particle meaning 'may it be' (a wish). تن و جان من lists two nouns 'my body and my life/soul' with من marking possession for both; the whole is a conventional formula 'may my body and soul be (sacrificed) to you'.
segments.s718-g68: به بیچارگی is again 'in/with misery' or 'into misery'; بر here functions as the preposition 'upon/on', linking that misfortune to جهانبان من ('my Jahanban' — either a title 'world-guardian' or a proper name). The sense is 'may my misery be upon my Jahanban' (i.e., let my suffering fall on my Jahanban).
segments.s718-g69: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is an archaic/past form 'he heard'. خراد برزین here is the proper name (Kharad Barzin); the name follows the verb, which is common in epic diction. دوید is the past 'ran' or 'hastened'. The clause thus means 'when Kharad Barzin heard, he ran.'
segments.s718-g70: ازان is a contraction of از آن 'from that'; خانه 'house'. تا indicates extent 'as far as / until'; پیش + خاتون = 'before/in the presence of the lady'; رسید is past 'arrived' or 'reached'. The whole segment: 'from that house until he reached the lady's presence.'
segments.s718-g71: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to her' (i.e., 'to her he said'). گفت is 'said' (here used simply as the reporting verb). کامد is an archaic form of 'آمد' meaning 'has come' or 'comes'; گه آرزوی is the noun phrase 'time/occasion of desire' = 'the wished-for time'. The clause reports: 'He said to her, "the wished-for time has come."'
segments.s718-g72: بگویم is first-person singular present/subjunctive ('I will say' / 'let me tell'); تو را is the object with the enclitic را marking 'you' as the addressee; ای is the vocative particle 'O' and زن نیک خوی is the addressed epithet 'woman of good disposition' (نیک خوی = kind/virtuous-tempered). The phrase is a direct address: 'I will tell you, O good-natured woman.'
segments.s718-g81: گل is the grammatical subject 'the flower'. اندر زمان is a temporal adverbial phrase 'at the time/when the time came' (اندر = 'in/at', زمان = 'time'). برنگینش is a pronominalized adjectival/nominal compound ('variegated/ornamented' with the third-person suffix -ش, 'its variegation/ornament'); نهاد is the past tense of نهادن 'put/placed' and here means 'put on' or 'assumed' (so the clause reads literally 'the flower, at the time, put on its variegation'). Word order is poetic S – temporal phrase – V with the pronominal object built into برنگینش.
segments.s718-g82: بیامد is a simple past 'came'. بران ('on/unto that' / 'thereupon') links this action to the previous clause. مرد جوینده is a noun phrase 'the man who is a seeker' (noun then participial/agentive modifier). داد is the past of دادن 'gave' and takes آن/برنگین (the thing previously mentioned) as its object; the implied subject is the flower (carried over from the previous clause). The clause therefore means 'it came and gave (that) to the seeking man.'
segments.s718-g83: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto it'. آفرین here is a noun meaning 'praise/acclamation' and with کرد ('did/made') forms the verbal phrase 'offered praise' (آفرین کردن = to praise). مرد دبیر is the subject 'the scribe-man' (mرد modified by دبیر); in poetic order the subject follows the verb phrase, so the clause reads 'the scribe praised him' or 'the scribe offered praise to him.'
segments.s718-g84: بیامد is 'came'. سپرد is the past of سپردن 'entrusted/handed over' (transitive). آن is the direct object pronoun 'that' (referring back to the thing given). بدین here functions as 'to this' (poetic contraction of به + این = 'to this'), marking the indirect object; it precedes مرد پیر 'the old man' which is the recipient. Overall: 'he came and entrusted that to this old man.' Note: the segment's internal split (ب + دین) reflects the cliticized/prepositional nature of 'به این' in the manuscript; دین as listed senses does not match this use, so the demonstrative sense is represented as the compound 'بدین.'
segments.s718-g33: نگه‌دار here functions as an address or imperative ('keeper' / 'keep watch'), followed by a prepositional/time phrase. از آن ماه بهرام روز literally gives the starting point/time: از = 'from', آن = 'that', ماه = 'month' (classical usage), بهرام names the month/astral name, and روز = 'day'. The whole segment establishes when/whom the injunction applies.
segments.s718-g34: برو is an imperative ('go'). تا introduces purpose/result here ('so that' / 'in order that'). در مرو is a locative phrase ('in Merv'), گیتی is the object 'the world', and فروز is a poetic root meaning 'to make bright / illuminate' used here as the resultive verb ('may make shine' or 'illumine'). The clause therefore expresses purpose: 'go so that you may illuminate the world in Merv.'
segments.s718-g35: وی is the subject pronoun 'he'. آن روز را uses the object/topic marker را to highlight 'that day'. شوم دارد به فال is an idiomatic predication: شوم = 'ill‑omened', دارد = 'has/holds' (present), and به فال = 'in/for an omen' (the idiom به فال داشتن = 'to take as an omen'). Word order puts the object/topic before the predicate for emphasis.
segments.s718-g36: نگه داشتیم is a compound past tense: نگه (keeping/watch) + داشتیم (we had/kept) → 'we kept watch' or 'we maintained (a watch)'. بسیار سال is an adverbial time phrase 'many years' (بسیار = 'many', سال = 'years') placed after the verb as a temporal complement.
segments.s718-g37: نخواهد که introduces a negative volitive/subjunctive clause ('he does not want that...'). انبوه is the predicate/adjective 'numerous' or 'a crowd', باشد is the subjunctive/optative form of 'to be' (expressing the desired/not‑desired state), and برش is an archaic/poetic expression meaning 'upon him' (بر + ش 'on/him'). The clause therefore means 'he does not want there to be a crowd upon him.'
segments.s718-g38: به دیبای چینی is an instrumental/with‑phrase: به = 'with, in', دیبای = 'brocade/silk', چینی = 'Chinese' (adjective). بپوشد is a subjunctive/future form of پوشیدن ('to put on / to wear'), and سرش = 'his head' (سر + ش). Grammatically this is a subordinate clause parallel to the previous one: 'nor that he should clothe his head in Chinese brocade.'
segments.s718-g39: چنین گوی is a poetic phrase 'thus says' / 'thus he said' (گوی as the act of saying). کز = 'that/from which' introduces source or content, and دخت خاقان پیام is a genitive sequence 'a message from the khaghan's daughter' (دخت = daughter, خاقان = khaghan/ruler, پیام = message). Together it reports the content/source of the speech: 'thus he said that (it is) a message from the khaghan's daughter.'
segments.s718-g40: رسانم is the verb 'to convey/deliver' used here in a context of reporting a message (sense: 'convey/deliver'). برین is the locative/dative compound ('بر' + 'ین' = 'to this'), so برین مهتر = 'to this chief'; شادکام is a predicate adjective 'glad/fortunate'. The clause functions as the report of what was conveyed: '(He) conveyed to this chief (that) be joyful' or 'he delivered to this chief the tidings, (be) joyful.'
segments.s718-g1: وزان is an adverbial particle 'from that/thereupon' placed before the clause. The core clause is 'بهرام شد روی' (literally 'Bahram became/turned face'), where شد is the inchoative/copular verb used with روی to mean 'turned his face' or 'proceeded toward.' تا + به + place (تا به مرو) marks the goal/direction 'as far as/to Merv.' Word order is adverbial → subject → (copula/verb) → directional phrase.
segments.s718-g2: بیاراست is a past/perfective verbal form of آراستن (here 'arrayed' or 'adorned') taking لشکر as its object ('arrayed the army'). چو introduces a simile ('as/like'); پر is an adjectival element 'full (of)'; تذرو is a poetic/lexical noun modifying the simile. The whole phrase modifies/characterizes the arrayed host.
segments.s718-g3: This is a simple main clause 'کس آمد به خاقان' = 'someone came to the khagan.' The following که begins reported content/indirect speech ('that ...'). از ترک و چین is a source phrase 'from the Turks and (from) China' attached to what will follow; و here is the coordinator 'and.'
segments.s718-g4: ممان‌تا functions as a notion of prevention/obstruction and here introduces the content of the report as a prohibitory idea ('prevent / do not allow'). کس آید is a subordinate clause 'that anyone come' (آید = come), and به ایران‌زمین marks the destination 'into the land of Iran.' The whole segment is reported instruction: 'prevent anyone from coming into Iran.'
segments.s718-g5: که introduces the reported clause. آگاهی ما is the direct object 'our information/news.' به خسرو برند uses برند (بردن) in a jussive/subjunctive or reported-instruction sense 'let them bring / that they bring' with به + proper name marking the indirect object ('to Khosrow'). Word order: object then indirect-object phrase then verbal form.
segments.s718-g6: ورا is the clitic/weak pronoun meaning 'to him' (indirect object). زان links back ('from/for that reason' or 'on account of that'), introducing the purpose/occasion. سخن here means 'message/report'; هدیه نو is a noun phrase 'a new gift'; برند repeats the verb 'bring' (same jussive/instructional force). The clause says they should also deliver a fresh gift to him because of that message.
segments.s718-g7: منادیگری (heralding/proclamation) is used as the action and کرد is the light verb 'did/made,' so 'خاقان چین منادیگری کرد' = 'the khagan of China made a proclamation / sent heralds.' Note the title 'خاقان چین' (khagan of China) is a nominal phrase following the verb phrase.
segments.s718-g8: که introduces the content of the proclamation. بی‌مهر is an epithet 'without favor / unkind / merciless' modifying ماکس (a proper name here). به ایران‌زمین is the destination phrase 'to the land of Iran.' Grammatically this is a reported clause stating who and whither (subject epithet + destination) as announced by the khagan.
segments.s718-g17: This is an SOV-style noun phrase headed by the second-person pronoun 'تو' (you/thou) followed by a series of objects joined with و. The nouns (نان جو ‘barley-bread’, ارزن ‘millet’, پوستین ‘fur coat’) are listed as what the subject has sought; there is no verb here yet because Persian (especially poetic) often places the object(s) before the verb.
segments.s718-g18: This segment contains the verbal/adverbial material that completes the clause. فراوان is an adverbial qualifier ('plentifully'), به before the verb is functioning adverbially, and جستی is the past second-person singular of جستن ('you sought/sought for'). ز هردر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from every door' and به چین uses به + چین where چین means 'fold' (i.e. piled/in folds). Together they mean 'you sought/plundered plentifully from every door, into folds.'
segments.s718-g19: کنون 'now' is a sentence-initial temporal adverb. خوردنیهات is a possessed plural noun 'your edibles/food' (خوردنی‌ها + the 2nd-person suffix), followed by a simple nominal predicate 'نان و بره' ('bread and lamb'). The copula 'is/are' is omitted (verbless predication), common in Persian.
segments.s718-g20: همان 'the same' links this clause to the previous (i.e. 'your covering likewise'). پوششت is a noun meaning 'your covering/garment' (root پوش 'cover'), جامه‌های is the plural 'garments/robes', and سره is an adjective 'pure/finely made' (here 'fine cloth'). The phrase is a nominal phrase describing the person's covering.
segments.s718-g21: چنان بود 'it was so' uses the past copula بود. یک چند is an idiomatic quantifier meaning 'for a while'/'for some time' (چند = a few/several). The coordinating و اکنون چنین contrasts the earlier state with the present: 'and now it is thus.'
segments.s718-g22: This is a pair of parallel exclamatory questions. چه before a noun is exclamatory/interrogative ('what/what sort of'), نفرین 'curse' is the object, شنیدی is the past second-person singular 'you heard', and the second clause repeats the pattern with آفرین 'praise/acclamation'. The verb شنیدی applies to both nouns in the parallel construction.
segments.s718-g23: کنون 'now' sets the time. روزگار تو 'your fortune/time' is the subject; بر is the preposition 'upon/on' and سرگذشت is a noun 'life-story' or 'one's life/fortunes'. The phrase indicates that the person's fortunes have been visited upon or have affected his life (a concise poetic expression rather than a literal verb 'passed').
segments.s718-g24: بسی روز و شب 'many/much days and nights' is a temporal quantity placed before the verb. دیدی is the past second-person singular 'you saw/you have seen'. The addition و کوه و دشت 'and mountain and plain' extends what you have seen; the whole is a compact past statement: 'you have seen many days and nights and mountains and plains.'
segments.s718-g57: همانا is an emphatic adverb ('verily/indeed') placed front for force. بتو is a prepositional phrase ('to you') preposed for emphasis; کس is the indefinite noun ('person/anyone') which here is negated by the verb. نپردازدی is an archaic negative verbal form meaning 'would not attend/come to' or 'would not pay heed' — used here in a classical sense to assert that no one would come to you.
segments.s718-g58: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying کس ('who/that'). با تو is 'with/against you' (here 'to/against you'), and بدانگه is a temporal adverb 'at that moment/thereupon.' بدی is the noun 'harm/evil' and سازدی is an archaic 2nd-person past/optative-form 'you would do/make' — together meaning 'who would at that time do you harm.'
segments.s718-g59: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدون is an archaic temporal adverb (here functioning like 'as yet / up to now'); که introduces the clause content. یابی is the present/subjunctive form 'you may/find' (classical use for potential), زکشتن is a prepositional phrase 'from killing', and رها is a predicate/adjective 'free/released' — together expressing 'if up to now you can/find release from killing.'
segments.s718-g60: جهان را uses را as the definite direct-object marker ('the world' as object). خریدی is the verb 'you would buy' (classical past/optative sense of acquiring), و simply 'and', دادی is 'you would give' (or 'you gave/would pay'), and بها is 'price/value' — yielding 'you would buy the world and pay its price.'
segments.s718-g61: تو را places the object/topic ('to you') before the subject for emphasis, را being the object marker. شاه پرویز is the subject ('King Parviz'), شهری is the object 'a city' (here used as the thing granted), and دهد is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive classical-form meaning 'gives/will give' — so 'King Parviz will give you a city.'
segments.s718-g62: همان picks up/points back to the same person ('that same [one]'). از جهان is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the world', نیز is 'also/likewise', بهری is a noun meaning 'a benefit/portion/share', and دهد again 'gives/will give' — together: 'the same (he) will also give a share/benefit of the world.'
segments.s718-g63: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the speech. گفت is the verb 'said' (classical past). با مرد دانا is a prepositional phrase 'to/with the wise man' (دانا = 'wise'), and قلون is an appositional name or epithet following the phrase — overall 'Thus he said to the wise man Qalon.'
segments.s718-g64: که introduces reported content ('that'). اکنون is 'now/at present'. بباید is the classical modal 'must/it is necessary' (3rd-person impersonal/modal use), یکی is 'one/a single' used here as 'one (person)', and رهنمون is the noun 'guide' — 'that now one guide is needed.'
segments.s718-g25: Word-for-word order: 'یکی کار دارم' = 'I have a certain/one matter' (subject implied 'I'). 'تو را' is the object with the enclitic object/contrast marker را after the pronoun (literally 'for you' / 'to you'). 'بیمناک' follows and functions as a vocative/adjective addressing 'تو' ('O fearful one').
segments.s718-g26: This is two conditional clauses compacted. 'اگرتخت یابی' = 'اگر تخت یابی' (if you attain/find the throne); 'یابی' is 2nd-person present/subjunctive ('may/you find' or 'you should find'). The following 'اگر تیره خاک' repeats the conditional particle 'اگر' and states a parallel/climactic condition 'if (the) earth (be) dark' — 'تیره خاک' = 'dark earth' (tira = dark/ominous + خاک = earth).
segments.s718-g27: 'ستانم' is 1st-person present/future-like form of ستاندن ('I take / I will exact'). 'یکی مهر' = 'one tribute/one payment' (a single item/sum). 'خاقان چین' is a genitive/compound: 'the Khagan of China' (title + place). The verb governs the whole object phrase: 'I will take one tribute from the Khagan of China.'
segments.s718-g28: 'چنان رو' uses an archaic/poetic imperative/jussive 'رو' (go/act thus) — here 'چنان' (so/thus) modifies the verb. 'که' introduces the result/clause ('so that...'). 'اندر نوردی زمین' = 'you traverse/roll over the earth' where 'نوردی' is a 2nd-person form of نوردن (to traverse/roll over). The clause therefore means 'go in such a way that you traverse the earth.'
segments.s718-g29: 'به نزدیک بهرام' is a temporal/locative phrase 'at/near the coming of Bahram' ('به' + 'نزدیک' + proper name). 'باید شدن' pairs the modal بایـد ('it is necessary') with the infinitive/verb شدن ('to become/happen'), producing the impersonal sense 'it must happen / it is necessary (when Bahram is near)'.
segments.s718-g30: This clause states the required behavior: 'به مروت فراوان' = 'with abundant chivalry/generosity' (به marking manner). 'بباید' is another modal 'one must' (present). The final word 'بدن' here functions as an archaic/poetic infinitive meaning 'to give/offer' (not the modern noun 'body'); the provided dictionary senses do not list this verb usage, so it is treated as archaic verbal usage.
segments.s718-g31: 'بپوشی' is the 2nd-person jussive/imperative of پوشیدن ('put on / wear') addressed to 'you'. 'همان' = 'that/same' modifies 'پوستین' (fur coat), and 'سیاه' = 'black' is the adjective. The phrase is an instruction: 'Wear that same black fur coat.'
segments.s718-g32: 'یکی کارد بستان' = 'take a certain/one knife' where 'بستان' is an imperative 'take!' (here used as a verb form, not the noun 'garden' listed). 'و بنورد راه' links with 'and' + an imperative-form 'بنورد' (go/traverse; verbal form of نوردن with the verbal prefix), followed by 'راه' (the road/path). Together: 'Take a knife and traverse the road.'
segments.s718-g9: شود is an optative/impersonal use of شدن meaning “may/let it happen.” تامیانش is the object (tamiyān- + -ash) meaning “its/ his entirety.” کنم is first‑person singular subjunctive/present “I make/do,” so شود ... کنم yields the sense “may it come about that I make…” بدو is the indirect object “to him/unto him.” نیم here is the lexical item ‘half’ (نیم) placed after the clause; in poetry it often sits metrically after the verb phrase and should be read as the noun “half.”
segments.s718-g10: به یزدان is an oath formula “by (the) God”: به as an adverbial/agent marker + یزدان. که here introduces the following exclamation/affirmation (“that/indeed”). نفروشم is the first‑person negative form of ‘to sell’ (literal negative present/subjunctive sense: “I will not sell”); او را is the definite object (he/him + را), and به سیم “for silver” (به = “for/to,” سیم = silver) states the price that will not be accepted.
segments.s718-g11: همی‌بود is an imperfect/continuous past form meaning “was / continued to be.” خراد here functions as an epithet meaning “wise” (an adjective/epithet attached to the name), برزین is the personal name, and سه ماه is a duration phrase “three months” placed after the name (Persian often places time phrases after the subject).
segments.s718-g12: همی‌داشت is the past continuous/resultative ‘he was holding/kept’ (had in his keeping). این رازها را is the direct object (این = “these / the foregoing,” رازها = “secrets,” را = object marker). نگاه here is a noun meaning “watch/view/keeping” (as in نگاه داشتن, “to keep/guard”), so the clause means he kept/guarded these secrets.
segments.s718-g13: به تنگی دل is an idiomatic prepositional phrase meaning “with constriction/oppression of the heart” (i.e., distressed or sorrowful). اندر is the older preposition “in/within,” followed by قلون with the object marker را (قلون را) — here قلون is treated as the person or thing summoned. بخواند is the past tense (or narrative preterite) of خواندن used in the sense “he called/summoned,” not just “read.”
segments.s718-g14: بران is a demonstrative/adverbial “to/onto that,” pointing to the place just mentioned. نامور is an adjective “famed/renowned,” جایگاهش is the noun “his place/seat” with the possessive suffix -ash, and نشاند is an archaic verb form meaning “(he) made sit / seated” (causative/past of نشستن). The clause describes setting him down in that famous seat.
segments.s718-g15: بدو is “to him” (dative/indirect object). گفت here heads the reported speech (“he said”), and روزی functions adverbially “one day / on a day” (classical Persian uses روزی to introduce episodic speech). که introduces the content clause that follows (“that”). کس در جهان = “anyone/person in the world.”
segments.s718-g16: ندارد is the present negative construction “does not have / there is not.” دلی + کش is a compound: دلی here = “of the heart / heart‑longing” (adjectival/nominal element) and کش is the agentive/relational element (as in compounds) forming something like “heart‑taking/heart‑longing” — together pointing to a hidden desire of the heart. نباشد is a negative subjunctive/optative here meaning “is not” / “may not be,” and نهان is the adjective “hidden/secret.” Grammatically the clause says no one in the world has a heart‑thing that is not hidden — i.e., everyone’s heart has hidden longing.
segments.s718-g73: ببند is an imperative 'shut/close'. اندرند is an archaic present-third‑plural meaning 'are inside' (اندر + اند). The noun phrase این دو کسهای من follows and identifies who is inside: این 'these', دو 'two' is included in the phrase text, کسهای (کس + های) is a plural 'persons/people', and من is the possessive sense 'mine' (object/possessive after the noun). The overall word order here is imperative verb + predicative clause + identifying NP.
segments.s718-g74: سزد is an impersonal present meaning 'it befits/it is proper'. What follows is a subordinate idea expressed without an explicit conjunction: گرگشاده (an adjectival/participial description 'wolf‑gaping, exposed to wolves') modifies the action expressed by کنی, the second‑person present(/subjunctive) form of کردن 'you do/make' (here 'you cause/expose'). پای من is the direct object 'my foot'. Grammatically: 'سزد' + subordinate clause with کنی (2nd person) + object.
segments.s718-g75: یکی here is an indefinite numeral 'one / a single'. مهر functions as 'a token of favour / a gift' (a small object marking favour). بستان in this construction is an imperative 'take/bring!' (verb) — note that the provided lemma senses were nominal, but in context بستان is the verb 'take/bring'. ز is the preposition 'from', خاقان is the proper name 'Khaghan', and مرا is split in the data as م + را but represents the object pronoun 'to me' (مرا) with را as object marker.
segments.s718-g76: چنان دان is an idiom meaning 'consider it so' or 'take it as such': چنان 'so' + دان (an imperative/stative 'consider'). که introduces the content clause. بخشیده‌ای is a perfect (2nd‑person) form meaning here 'you have given/bestowed' (classical use where 'give' = بخشیدن); جان مرا 'my life' is the direct object, with را again marking definiteness/emphasis. Together: 'Consider it as if you had given me my life.'
segments.s718-g77: بدو is a prepositional/dative phrase 'to him' (ب + او contracted), here marking the indirect object. گفت is the verb 'said' (past 3rd singular) — the provided senses list was nouny, but in context it is the verb. خاتون is the subject 'the lady'. که introduces reported speech or a subordinate clause. خفتست is an archaic present 'is asleep' (خفت + است fused), and مست is the predicate adjective 'drunk/intoxicated'.
segments.s718-g78: مگر here functions as a rhetorical/interrogative particle ('shall I not / why not...?') introducing a proposed action. (The clause contains the verb نهم 'I put' which appears in the segment text but is not listed as a separate token in the provided word list.) گل is the object 'a flower/rose', از نگینش is a prepositional phrase 'from her gem' (نگین + possessive suffix -ش = 'her'), and بدست literally 'into (my) hand' or 'to hand' (directional result). The whole is a rhetorical question meaning 'Shall I not take a flower from her jewel into my hand?'
segments.s718-g79: ز is the preposition 'from'. خراد here can function as a proper name or noun 'Kharad / wisdom' depending on context; in this line it works as the source 'from Kharad'. برزین is the subject name 'Barzin'. گل مهر is a compound 'flower of favour/love' (گل + مهر) — a token — and خواست is the past form 'wanted/asked for'. Word order places the source prepositional phrase before the subject, then the object and verb.
segments.s718-g80: به is a directional preposition 'to / toward'. بالین here means 'by the bedside / at the pillow' (بالین can be 'pillow' or 'bedside' — here 'bedside'). مست is the noun/adjective 'the drunk (person)' (the one who is intoxicated). آمد is simple past 'came'. از حجره راست is a phrase 'straight from the chamber/room' where راست is an adverb/adjunct meaning 'straight/directly' modifying آمد (came directly).

sentences.s718-s25: Drive the knife in and cut his navel right through; and from that, then, you will find a passage to the wet‑nurse.
sentences.s718-s26: Whoever hears his voice will, before the spahbed, go to the very end of the smoke.
sentences.s718-s27: One (goes) toward the carpet and another toward the treasure — no suffering will come to you from killing.
sentences.s718-s28: And if they themselves kill you, you will be worldly‑wise; you have approved (or come to accept) both the good and the bad.
sentences.s718-s21: Keep that same dagger bare in your sleeve until you summon him alone.
sentences.s718-s22: When you come near the wooden platform, go up and say thus to that proud maiden.
sentences.s718-s23: He said to me: when you whisper a secret into the ear, hide those words from strangers.
sentences.s718-s24: If she asks, 'What is the secret?', tell her 'Come to me.' Hurry, and go near Bahram.
sentences.s718-s33: Truly, a hundred years have come upon me; how much longer shall I endure this misery?
sentences.s718-s34: May my body and soul be sacrificed for you; may my misery fall upon my Jahanban.
sentences.s718-s35: When Kharad Barzin heard, he ran; from that house he hastened until he reached the lady's presence.
sentences.s718-s36: He said to her, 'The wished-for time has come; I will tell you, O woman of good disposition.'
sentences.s718-s41: At the appointed time the flower put on its variegated colors, and it came and gave them to that searching man.
sentences.s718-s42: To him the scribe offered praise; then he came and entrusted that to this old man.
sentences.s718-s17: Keep watch from that Bahram‑month day; go so that the world in Merv may shine.
sentences.s718-s18: He regards that day as ill‑omened in divination; we kept watch for many years.
sentences.s718-s19: He does not wish that a crowd be upon him, nor that he should cover his head with Chinese brocade.
sentences.s718-s20: Thus he said: that from the khaghan's daughter I am messenger; bring this chief joyful.
sentences.s718-s1: From that, Bahram turned his face toward Merv and arrayed his host as if full and scattering.
sentences.s718-s2: Someone came to the khagan and reported that he should prevent anyone from the Turks and China from coming into the land of Iran.
sentences.s718-s3: that they bring our intelligence to Khosrow, and to him, because of that message, bring a new gift.
sentences.s718-s4: The khagan of China sent heralds, proclaiming that the merciless Max (Maks) should go to the land of Iran.
sentences.s718-s9: You sought plentiful barley-bread, millet, and fur coats, gathering them from every door in folds.
sentences.s718-s10: Now your food is bread and lamb, and your covering (too) is garments of fine cloth.
sentences.s718-s11: It was so for a while, and now it is thus; what curses did you hear, and what praise?
sentences.s718-s12: Now your fortunes have passed over your life; you have seen many days and nights and mountains and plains.
sentences.s718-s29: Truly, no one would come to you who would at that moment do you harm.
sentences.s718-s30: If, up to now, you could find release from killing, you would buy the world and pay its price.
sentences.s718-s31: King Parviz will grant you a city, and likewise will give you a share of the world.
sentences.s718-s32: Thus he said to the wise man Qalon: now one guide is needed.
sentences.s718-s13: I have a certain task for you, O fearful one; if you should attain the throne, if the earth grow dark.
sentences.s718-s14: I will exact one tribute from the Khagan of China; go so that you traverse the earth.
sentences.s718-s15: When Bahram approaches it will be necessary to go; you must act with abundant generosity.
sentences.s718-s16: Put on that same black fur coat; take a knife and traverse the road.
sentences.s718-s5: May it be that I make him wholly his; by God, I will not sell him for silver.
sentences.s718-s6: For three months the wise Barzin remained there; he kept these secrets in his charge.
sentences.s718-s7: With a sore (constricted) heart he summoned Qalun and set him in that famed place.
sentences.s718-s8: He said to him one day: there is no one in the world whose heart‑longing is not concealed.
sentences.s718-s37: Shut them in — these two of mine are inside; it is fitting that you expose my foot to the wolves.
sentences.s718-s38: Bring me one token of favour from the Khaghan; consider it so — as if you had given me my life.
sentences.s718-s39: The lady said to him that she sleeps drunk — 'Shall I not put a flower from her jewel into my hand?'
sentences.s718-s40: Barzin asked Kharad for the love‑flower; he came straight from the chamber to the drunk one's bedside.
